questions.to_find: The output printed by the given C program involving pointer arithmetic and dereferencing.

understand.plain: The program declares an integer x=1 and an array z={10,11}. It uses a pointer p to first modify x to 10, then uses pointer arithmetic to add 3 to z[1], making z[1]=14. Finally it prints x, z[0], and z[1].

keywords.explain: Accessing or modifying the value stored at the memory address held by pointer p.
Adding an integer to a pointer moves it forward by that many elements of its base type in memory.
keywords.example: &z[0] + 1 gives the address of z[1], since z is an int array.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='30' width='100' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='70' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>x</text><text x='70' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>10</text><rect x='160' y='30' width='100' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>z[0]</text><text x='210' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>10</text><rect x='300' y='30' width='100' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='350' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>z[1]</text><text x='350' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>14</text><text x='20' y='120' class='sub ink'>*p=10 sets x=10</text><text x='20' y='140' class='sub ink'>*(&z[0]+1)+=3 means z[1]=11+3=14</text><text x='20' y='160' class='sub ink'>Output: 10, 10, 14</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Memory layout showing x=10, z[0]=10, z[1]=14 after pointer operations. Pointer p first modifies x to 10, then &z[0]+1 addresses z[1] and adds 3 making it 14.

given.aim: Trace pointer operations and determine the final printed values of x, z[0], and z[1].

terms.meaning: Initial values of the integer variable x and two-element array z.
terms.connects: These variables are modified through pointer operations during the program.

terms.meaning: p is set to point to x, then *p=10 writes 10 into x through the pointer.
terms.example: After these two lines, x becomes 10.
terms.connects: Changes x from 1 to 10; z remains unchanged at this point.

terms.meaning: &z[0]+1 is the address of z[1] (pointer arithmetic); dereferencing and adding 3 sets z[1] = 11+3 = 14.
terms.example: If base address of z is 100, &z[0]+1 = 104 for int, which is &z[1].
terms.connects: This is the key operation that changes z[1] from 11 to 14.

given.plan: Step through each pointer assignment and dereference in order. Track the current value of x, z[0], and z[1] after each modification. Note that the assignment p = &z[1] does not modify z[1] by itself; the next line *(&z[0]+1) += 3 does the actual modification.

steps.title: Trace pointer operations on x
steps.formula_raw: *p = value  =>  variable pointed to by p gets value
steps.apply:
Initially: x=1, z[0]=10, z[1]=11.
p = &x  (p now holds address of x)
*p = 10  (x is set to 10)
Now: x=10, z[0]=10, z[1]=11.
Next: p = &z[1]  (p now holds address of z[1], but no write yet)
steps.note: The assignment p = &z[1] just repositions p; it does NOT change z[1].

steps.title: Evaluate *(&z[0] + 1) += 3
steps.apply:
&z[0] is the address of z[0].
&z[0] + 1 advances the pointer by sizeof(int), landing on z[1].
*(&z[0]+1) = z[1] = 11.
z[1] += 3  =>  z[1] = 11 + 3 = 14.
Final state: x=10, z[0]=10, z[1]=14.

steps.title: Read the printf output
steps.note: Options A and B have x=1, which is wrong. Option C has z[0]=14, which is wrong. Only D matches.

questions.formula_note: Pointer arithmetic: adding integer n to a pointer of type T* advances the address by n * sizeof(T) bytes, moving to the n-th next element. Dereferencing then reads/writes that element.

questions.to_find: The two integers printed: the pointer difference (q-p) and the value difference (*q - *p), both cast to int.

understand.plain: The program creates a double array with values 20.0 and 25.0. Pointer p points to the first element and q points to the second element (p+1). It then prints (q-p) which is the element count between them, and (*q - *p) which is the numerical difference between the values they point to.

keywords.explain: Subtracting two pointers of the same type gives the number of elements between them, not the byte difference.
keywords.example: If p = &a[0] and q = &a[1], then q - p = 1 (one double element apart), not 8 (bytes apart).

keywords.explain: Dereferencing q and p gives the actual double values stored at those addresses; their difference is a numerical subtraction.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='460' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='40' y='40' width='140' height='60' class='hi'/><text x='110' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a[0]</text><text x='110' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>20.0</text><rect x='220' y='40' width='140' height='60' class='bg'/><text x='290' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a[1]</text><text x='290' y='88' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>25.0</text><text x='110' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>p (p=a=&a[0])</text><text x='290' y='125' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>q (q=p+1=&a[1])</text><text x='40' y='150' class='sub ink'>q-p=1 (element diff), *q-*p=5.0 -> (int)5</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array a with a[0]=20.0 and a[1]=25.0. Pointer p points to a[0], pointer q points to a[1]. The element-count difference q-p=1, and the value difference *q-*p=5.

given.aim: Compute (q-p) as an element count and (*q - *p) as a value difference, both cast to int.

terms.meaning: A double array of size 2. Each double occupies 8 bytes on typical platforms.
terms.example: a[0]=20.0 at some address, a[1]=25.0 at address+8.
terms.connects: The values 20.0 and 25.0 are what *p and *q will eventually dereference to.

terms.meaning: p points to a[0]; q points to a[1] (one double-element beyond p).
terms.example: If p is at address 1000, q is at address 1008 (sizeof(double)=8).
terms.connects: The difference q-p in pointer arithmetic = 1 element, not 8 bytes.

terms.meaning: Pointer subtraction returns a ptrdiff_t (element count). Here it equals 1 since q is one element ahead of p.
terms.example: q - p = 1 regardless of the size of double.

given.plan: Evaluate q-p using pointer arithmetic rules (result = number of elements, not bytes). Evaluate *q - *p as floating-point subtraction of the stored values, then cast to int. Print both.

steps.title: Pointer difference q - p
steps.formula_raw: (q - p) = number of elements of type T between q and p
steps.apply:
p = &a[0], q = &a[1]
q - p = 1 (one double element apart)
(int)(q - p) = 1
steps.note: Pointer difference is in units of the pointed-to type (double), NOT bytes. Do not confuse with byte difference (which would be 8).

steps.title: Value difference *q - *p
steps.note: The cast to int truncates fractional part; here 5.0 becomes 5 exactly.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply:
Output: 1, 5
Option A (4,8): wrong — 4 and 8 are byte-level values, not element/value differences.
Option B (1,5): correct.
Option C (8,5): 8 would be sizeof(double), not pointer difference in elements.
Option D (1,8): 8 is not the value difference.
steps.note: A common mistake is computing byte-level pointer difference (8) instead of element-level difference (1).

questions.formula_note: Pointer subtraction in C yields the number of array elements between the two pointers (not byte count). Value difference is straightforward floating-point arithmetic on the dereferenced values.

questions.to_find: The integer value printed by the program after the function foo modifies a through a pointer.

understand.plain: The program declares variables a=20 and b=25. Pointer z is set to point to a. Function foo is called with z (which holds &a) and b (value 25). Inside foo, *p = x writes 25 into the memory location p points to, which is a. So a becomes 25 and that is what gets printed.

keywords.explain: Passing a pointer to a function allows the function to modify the original variable through dereferencing.
keywords.example: foo(z, b) where z = &a means foo can change a via *p = x.

keywords.explain: Inside foo, p receives the value of z (which is &a). So *p accesses and modifies a directly.
keywords.example: *p = x inside foo is equivalent to a = b = 25 from main's perspective.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='460' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='120' height='55' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a (before)</text><text x='80' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>20</text><rect x='200' y='20' width='120' height='55' class='hi'/><text x='260' y='42' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>a (after)</text><text x='260' y='66' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>25</text><rect x='20' y='100' width='120' height='55' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='122' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>b</text><text x='80' y='146' text-anchor='middle' class='big ink'>25</text><text x='20' y='180' class='sub ink'>z = &a; foo(z,b) => *p=x => a=25</text><text x='20' y='196' class='sub ink'>printf prints a = 25</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Variable a starts at 20. Pointer z holds address of a. foo is called with p=z=&a and x=25. Inside foo, *p=x sets a=25. printf then prints 25.

given.aim: Determine the value of a after foo modifies it through a pointer, and what printf prints.

terms.meaning: Pointer z is assigned the address of variable a, so z and &a are the same pointer value.
terms.example: If a is at address 2000, then z = 2000.
terms.connects: When foo(z, b) is called, parameter p in foo receives value 2000, i.e., p points to a.

terms.term: foo(z, b): p = z, x = b
terms.meaning: foo receives a copy of z (which is &a) as p, and the value of b (25) as x.
terms.connects: The assignment *p = x writes 25 at the address of a, changing a's value.

terms.meaning: Writes the value of x (25) into the memory location that p points to (which is a in main).
terms.connects: After foo returns, a = 25 in main, so printf("%d", a) prints 25.

given.plan: Trace how pointer z carries the address of a into function foo. Inside foo, *p = x writes the value of x to the memory location p points to (which is a). After foo returns, read a's updated value.

steps.title: Set up pointer and initial values
steps.formula_raw: z = &a  =>  z holds the address of a
steps.apply:
a = 20, b = 25
z = &a  (z now points to a)
foo(z, b) is called: parameter p = z = &a, parameter x = b = 25
steps.note: C is pass-by-value, so p gets a copy of z's value (&a). This still allows modification of a.

steps.title: Execute foo and trace the effect on a
steps.apply:
Inside foo: p = &a, x = 25
*p = x  =>  *(&a) = 25  =>  a = 25
foo returns.
steps.note: The change to *p persists in main because p points to main's variable a.

steps.title: Read the printf output
steps.apply:
a = 25 after foo returns.
printf("%d", a) prints 25.
steps.note: The original value 20 was overwritten through the pointer; 25 is printed.

questions.formula_note: Pass-by-pointer in C: passing a pointer allows the called function to modify the caller's variable by dereferencing the pointer. *p = x inside the function is equivalent to assigning to the original variable in the caller.
